topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a monotonous soundtrack to my anxiety. I smoothed down my already perfectly pressed blazer for the tenth time, my fingers tracing the fine wool as if seeking some anchor. Across the small table, Liam was laughing, recounting a story about a weekend trip, his easy confidence a stark contrast to the tightness in my chest. When the manager finally called our names, my stomach performed a slow, sickening flip. I watched Liam’s relaxed posture, the way he leaned back, seemingly unburdened. My palms grew slick; I could feel the heat rising up my forearms. I offered a tight, practiced smile, the kind that didn't quite reach my eyes, and waited for the verdict, every muscle in my body coiled, ready to spring, or perhaps just to collapse.

[story 2]

"You seem a little pale, Maya," Liam commented, nudging my arm with his elbow. His tone was light, almost dismissive, which only amplified the frantic fluttering inside me. I forced a bright, airy laugh, the sound feeling brittle in the quiet room. I kept glancing at the application form lying between us, the paper suddenly feeling heavy, an insurmountable obstacle. Every breath felt shallow, as if the air itself was too thin to sustain me. When the hiring manager asked about my long-term vision, I stammered, my mind a blank slate. I desperately wanted him to see the competence I possessed, but all that emerged was a shaky murmur, a plea for reassurance that I wasn't about to fail spectacularly.

[story 3]

I reread the job description for the third time, the words blurring into meaningless symbols. The silence in the waiting room stretched into an unbearable expanse. Liam was across the aisle, tapping his pen rhythmically against his notepad, a picture of calm preparedness. I kept fiddling with the strap of my handbag, a nervous habit I couldn't seem to shake. When it was my turn, my throat constricted. I could feel the pressure building behind my eyes, a physical weight pressing down on my vision. I managed to articulate my qualifications, but my voice cracked midway through, a thin, reedy sound that betrayed the carefully constructed facade I was trying to maintain. I just wanted the outcome to be decided for me, to have someone else take the lead.
